text: I'm going to speak to you over the next short while about laparoscopic total proctocolectomy and brook ileostomy in the treatment of inflammatory bowel disease. The indications for this operation are several, in general being far more common in patients with Crohn's disease than in patients with mucosal cervical colitis or familial adenomatous polyposis. But certainly the operation can be used in both groups of patients. And the patients with Crohn's disease, in general, they're patients whose rectum is pretty much unusable, poor capacity, poor compliance from chronic inflammation, and or severe perianal disease. Once in a while, someone with bad proctitis with no perianal disease may prefer a subtotal colectomy, ileostomy, see if we can aggressively treat the rectum, with some topical compounds and then do an ilioproctostomy at a subsequent date. But if the patient also has severe perianal disease, unfortunately that ends up being an interesting teric dissection. There is also the indication in patients who have a pretty reasonable rectum, but a typical watering pot perineum with severe fistulizing disease where total proctocolectomy ends up being the operation of choice. In patients with ulcerative colitis, it is seldom that they would select a total proctocolectomy if truly well-informed about all the options, but there are some instances. Patients with very low resting pressures who may be incontinent may prefer that option. Patients who are very elderly may not have the sort of stamina, if you will, to withstand the operation of a J-pouch if that operation were accompanied by any postoperative adverse sequelae. And then there's the group in whom you try a pouch and you just simply can't make one, but the patient didn't want a continental ileostomy. Then there's this odd group of indeterminate colitis, although that's far less seldom. There are a couple of studies looking at total abdominal colectomies performed laparoscopically, as well as pouches performed laparoscopically. There's not a huge amount in the literature, nothing like the volume we see for sigmoid colitis, ileocolic resection for Crohn's, cancer operations. fairly less common in large measure due to the magnitude of the operation and the technical difficulties attended with it. We had published in nineteen ninety-two on laparoscopic total abdominal colectomies that we'd started performing sixteen years ago in nineteen ninety-one and in this initial study we discussed a group of patients who'd undergone either standard laparotomy with total abdominal colectomy and ileoprotostomy or ilioanal reservoir and we looked at a similar albeit quite small group at the time in nineteen ninety two publications a group who underwent the same operations but performed laparoscopically in that very early study from nineteen ninety one operations performed laparoscopically we looked at the typical parameters for length of surgery length of ileus length of hospitalization morbidity and mortality And again, they were small groups, five patients in each group, pretty well matched by age, by gender, as well as by indication for surgery. The two patients with colonic inertia underwent abdominal colectomelial proctostomy and not pouched. And in that early group, you can see that the length of surgery was longer laparoscopically. The length of ileus was not any better. The length of hospital stay was not really dramatically better, and there was really no significant morbidity or mortality. So at that point, we were able to say it seems feasible. We don't seem to be doing the patients any significant harm, no recognizable benefits, but at least it was kind of a green light to continue on.

A decade later, there's obviously a larger series in the literature, and this one from the Journal of Sages, Surgical Endoscopy, included seventy-three patients who underwent total abdominal colectomy with iliorectal, performed laparoscopic or open, as well as twenty-eight, again a much smaller group, undergoing the operation we're primarily discussing this morning, total proctoclectomy and brook ileostomy, fifteen laparoscopic, thirteen open, with no differences in gender, age, or weight in either of the two groups. If we look at the laparoscopic versus open, specifically in the total proctocolectomy series, we have twenty-eight patients in total, no differences, again, gender, no differences in age, median weight, or indication for surgery being almost evenly distributed between Crohn's disease and mucosal sort of colitis. The operative time was still much longer, as we'd found it a decade earlier, a mean of four hundred minutes and up to ten hours. Now again, this is one of the reasons that we don't see, or one of several reasons, we don't see as many series in the literature on laparoscopic total proctocolectomy as we see on laparoscopic sigmoid or laparoscopic ileocolic. Most people can't afford to spend eight to ten hours in the OR and shouldn't. And most operating rooms wouldn't generally allow it from a cost point of view, if nothing else. And what's more, at the end of it, if we look at the length of stay, it's eight days instead of nine days. It's not a dramatic gain like we see in other areas. Granted, we're not seemingly doing any harm to the patients in terms of an increased incidence of perioperative complications or mortality. but the length of stay is just not dramatically decreased. If we look at the patients available for follow-up and we look at the median follow-up, the satisfaction with the operation, short-term and long-term complications, again, they're not dramatically different between the two groups. So it becomes somewhat hard to justify doing the operation other than to say we can't. Now, of course, the caveat is if you do a lot of these, if you happen to have a practice that is a tertiary or floral inflammatory bowel disease practice, and you're doing a lot of these, you may be able to get a different result than if you're doing the occasional total proctocolectomy, prokiliostomy. I'll show you some technical facets of the operation momentarily that do make it nicely suited for laparoscopy if you're in the vicinity of doing fifty to a hundred or more ulcerative colitis and Crohn's patients per year with either J-pouch or total proctocolectomy. This is Peter Marcello's paper from Disease of the Colon and Rectum, published the same year as the surgical endoscopy study, and these patients underwent total colectomy. for colitis in the acute setting. Slightly different group of patients than in the chronic setting. The chronic patients tending to be more refractory to medical management, the acute obviously being by definition acutely ill. But you can see the methodology in the manuscript. No differences, again, in age, in the gender distribution, in the body mass index, or in the diagnosis. And one thing I will draw your attention to, that in many of the laparoscopic series, the patients tend to be on the thin side. Mean BMI of twenty-four, they're reasonably well selected cases. It's not a mean of thirty, as we might see if we just randomly selected patients from any of our practices in North America or in Western Europe. The operative time, and this is in the hands of a master laparoscopic surgeon, somebody very much respected by this society and others, but still the operative time is virtually doubled, even in those hands. The return of bowel function, a little bit quicker, and length of stay is two days less. So there's some gain here, but at what price, doubling the length of OR, I'm not sure.

The surgeon uses a peri-umbilical port for laparoscopic total proctocolectomy, moving the camera frequently. Babcock forceps are used to identify the ureter in the right lower quadrant, while a 5mm harmonic scalpel is employed for the line of TOLT mobilization. The patient's head is positioned towards the right side of the screen, and the feet towards the left, with the right ureter reflected down.

The surgeon uses multiple ports for laparoscopic dissection, including left and right upper and lower quadrant ports, to facilitate mobilization of the colon. The harmonic scalpel is used to dissect around the hepatic flexure, with the right ureter visible and cleared of surrounding tissue. The ENSEAL instrument is employed to divide the gastrocolic omentum, and the transverse mesocolon is taken with an endoscopic linear cutter. The spleen and splenic flexure are visible as the surgeon mobilizes the left colon using the harmonic scalpel, ensuring the left ureter is exposed. The procedure involves intracorporeal dissection with energy sources, focusing on the colon wall rather than proximal vessels, and concludes with mobilization of the entire abdominal colon.

The surgeon encounters issues with the stapler and clip, opting to use an endo-loop for hemostasis. The dissection proceeds into the pelvis and pre-sacral space, with the harmonic scalpel used to navigate the avascular plane in front of the sacrum and behind the mesorectum. The surgeon carefully reflects a nerve laterally and manages the large uterus with a clamp to maintain visualization. The rectum is transected, and the specimen is extracted through the perineum, facilitating the creation of a tension-free ileostomy. The operation concludes with the maturation of the brook ileostomy.
